papers.title: Engaging the head and the heart: why scientists turn to poetry
papers.authors: Josie Glausiusz
papers.summary: Scientists use poetry to visualize complex problems and communicate science to a wider audience.

papers.title: ‘Yes, we can’: a blueprint for a clean economy and healthy society
papers.authors: Andrew Macintosh
papers.summary: An economist proposes a path to a more efficient, prosperous, and sustainable world by transitioning away from oil and gas markets.

papers.title: Why the US needs a unified, mission-based strategy for health innovation
papers.authors: Victor J. Dzau, Melissa H. Laitner, Emily L. Shambaugh, Alex M. Helman
papers.summary: A unified, mission-based strategy for health innovation in the US would address unmet medical needs, reduce costs, and improve treatment reach.

papers.title: Moon fly-by live coverage: Artemis crew enter ‘nail-biting’ phase as they lose comms
papers.authors: Alexandra Witze
papers.summary: Astronauts on the Artemis mission are flying by the Moon's far side and setting records, with scientists monitoring their progress.

papers.title: What Artemis II’s astronauts will look for on the Moon’s far side
papers.authors: Alexandra Witze
papers.summary: Mission scientists discuss the geological features they're most excited for the Artemis II crew to observe on the Moon's far side.

papers.title: Massive budget cuts for US science proposed again by Trump administration
papers.authors: Max Kozlov, Dan Garisto, Edward Chen
papers.summary: The Trump administration has proposed massive budget cuts for US science, which would also curb federal payments for scientific publishing.

papers.title: Regular physical activity in midlife cuts risk of early death
papers.authors: Unknown authors
papers.summary: Regular physical activity in midlife reduces the risk of early death, with even a few hours of moderate-to-vigorous exercise per week having a strong effect.

papers.title: Artemis II is go: humans head to the Moon after half-century absence
papers.authors: Benjamin Thompson, Alexandra Witze
papers.summary: The Artemis II mission will send humans to the Moon for the first time in over half a century, with a focus on scientific research and exploration.

papers.title: The Moon belongs to all of us — not just countries that can afford to reach it
papers.authors: Moriba Jah
papers.summary: The Moon should be considered a shared resource for humanity, rather than a privilege reserved for countries that can afford to explore it.

papers.title: ‘Treasure trove’ of antiviral proteins could inspire powerful molecular tools
papers.authors: Miryam Naddaf
papers.summary: Researchers have identified a 'treasure trove' of antiviral proteins in bacteria, which could inspire powerful molecular tools and biotechnologies.

papers.title: How procrastination can rob you of career fulfilment in science
papers.authors: Holly Newson
papers.summary: This paper discusses how procrastination can hinder career fulfillment in science, suggesting that fear of failure or success may be underlying causes.

papers.title: ‘It’s a real shock’: quantum-computing breakthroughs pose imminent risks to cybersecurity
papers.authors: Davide Castelvecchi
papers.summary: Quantum computing breakthroughs pose a risk to cybersecurity, as quantum computers may be able to crack security keys and cryptocurrencies.

papers.title: China is planning to land people on the Moon — and might beat the United States to it
papers.authors: Jonathan O’Callaghan
papers.summary: China plans to land people on the Moon by 2030 and build a permanent base, potentially beating the United States to it.

papers.title: Mix-and-match synthesis of 3D small molecules
papers.authors: Huyen N. Nguyen, Daniel J. Blair
papers.summary: A new chemistry method enables the modular synthesis of 3D organic molecules, which could be useful for drug discovery and materials design.

papers.title: Breakthrough computer chip tech could help meet ‘monumental demand’ driven by AI
papers.authors: Katherine Bourzac
papers.summary: A new computer chip technology uses giant mirrors to produce tiny transistors and circuits, which could help meet the demand driven by AI.

papers.title: Why some cancer-fighting immune cells lose their strength inside tumours
papers.authors: Edward Chen
papers.summary: Researchers found that mitochondria play a crucial role in powering dendritic cells, which are immune defenders. However, these cells can lose their strength when inside tumors.

papers.title: Structural modifications in strain-engineered bilayer nickelate thin films
papers.authors: Lopa Bhatt, Edgar Abarca Morales, Abigail Y. Jiang, Eun Kyo Ko, Yi-Feng Zhao et al. (+11 more)
papers.summary: Scientists engineered bilayer nickelate thin films and observed structural modifications. This research could lead to advancements in materials science.

papers.title: These scientists chased a jet to learn more about ‘lean-burn’ contrails
papers.authors: Benjamin Thompson, Shamini Bundell
papers.summary: Researchers conducted an in-flight experiment to study contrails and found that reducing soot emissions may not necessarily decrease their formation. This suggests the importance of considering multiple factors when addressing environmental issues.

papers.title: These advanced solar cells have an antique source: old bullets
papers.authors: Unknown authors
papers.summary: Scientists discovered that old bullets can be recycled to create raw materials for advanced solar cells, specifically perovskite solar cells.

papers.title: DNA damage burden causes selective CUX2 neuron loss in neuroinflammation
papers.authors: Laura Morcom, Wenlong Xia, Zhaoyang Xu, Yashika Awasthi, Celine Geywitz et al. (+33 more)
papers.summary: Researchers found that DNA damage burden and inadequate repair contribute to selective vulnerability in neuroinflammatory injury, specifically affecting CUX2+ cortical layer 2/3 excitatory neurons.

papers.title: Entanglement and electronic coherence in attosecond molecular photoionization
papers.authors: L.-M. Koll, A. J. Suñer-Rubio, T. Witting, R. Y. Bello, A. Palacios et al. (+2 more)
papers.summary: A study on attosecond molecular photoionization shows that entanglement influences electronic coherence in H2+ molecules.

papers.title: The 1000 Chinese Pangenome empowers medical and population genetics
papers.authors: Yifei Wang, Zhongqu Duan, Dan Chen, Dandan Shi, Yi Ding et al. (+16 more)
papers.summary: The 1000 Chinese Pangenome project generated a comprehensive resource of medically relevant genic variations.

papers.title: Gene regulatory landscape dissected by single-cell four-omics sequencing
papers.authors: Yujie Chen, Zhiyuan Liu, Heming Xu, Jiayu Liu, Mengxuan Wang et al. (+6 more)
papers.summary: A single-cell four-omics sequencing study revealed dynamics and spatial clustering of epigenome profiles across cell types and tissues.

papers.title: Developmental organization of sensory and sympathetic ganglia
papers.authors: Keng Ioi Vong, Yanina D. Alvarez, Qingquan Zhang, Jiaming Weng, Geoffroy Noel et al. (+16 more)
papers.summary: A study on neural crest fate bias found that it predominantly emerges within the neural tube.

papers.title: Dopaminergic mechanisms of dynamical social specialization
papers.authors: C. Solié, A. Nicolson, R. Justo, Y. Layadi, B. Morin et al. (+19 more)
papers.summary: A study on dopaminergic mechanisms found that they drive sex-divergent patterns of social specialization in mice.

papers.title: A µ-opioid receptor superagonist analgesic with minimal adverse effects
papers.authors: Juan L. Gomez, Emilya N. Ventriglia, Zachary J. Frangos, Agnieszka Sulima, Michael J. Robertson et al. (+39 more)
papers.summary: A new opioid receptor superagonist has been developed with minimal adverse effects. This compound, N-desethyl-fluornitrazene, is derived from nitazenes and shows supramaximal intrinsic efficacy in rodent models.

papers.title: Moiré engineering of Cooper-pair density modulation states
papers.authors: Zihao Wang, Bing Xia, Stephen Paolini, Zi-Jie Yan, Pu Xiao et al. (+8 more)
papers.summary: Researchers have engineered a moiré superlattice in Sb2Te3/FeTe bilayers, creating spatially modulated superconducting gaps that can be tuned by replacing Sb2Te3 with Bi2Te3.

papers.title: Electric dipole moment drives the dynamics of the TNFR1 complex I signalosome
papers.authors: Jianping Liu, Jing Zhao, Jiayang Gao, Kun Zhao, Yaoyao Han et al. (+13 more)
papers.summary: Electric dipole moments play a key role in driving the assembly and disassembly of super-signalling complex I for promoting NF-κB signalling.

papers.title: Evidence of the pair-instability gap from black-hole masses
papers.authors: Hui Tong, Maya Fishbach, Eric Thrane, Matthew Mould, Thomas A. Callister et al. (+11 more)
papers.summary: LIGO–Virgo–KAGRA’s fourth Gravitational-Wave Transient Catalog shows evidence of a clear pair-instability gap in the distribution of binary black-hole secondary masses.

papers.title: Expansion of outer cortical CUX2 neurons requires adaptations for DNA repair
papers.authors: Wenlong Xia, Laura Morcom, Zhaoyang Xu, I-Ling Lu, Qing Wang et al. (+15 more)
papers.summary: The transcription factor ATF4 regulates double-stranded DNA repair within vulnerable CUX2+ upper-layer 2/3 cortical neurons, enabling their survival during development.

papers.title: AhR inhibition promotes axon regeneration via a stress–growth switch
papers.authors: Dalia Halawani, Yiqun Wang, Jiaxi Li, Daniel Halperin, Haofei Ni et al. (+7 more)
papers.summary: Researchers found that inhibiting a protein called AhR promotes axon regeneration by switching from stress adaptation to repair. This discovery could lead to new treatments for nerve damage and paralysis.

papers.title: Substantial aircraft contrail formation at low soot emission levels
papers.authors: Christiane Voigt, Raphael Märkl, Daniel Sauer, Rebecca Dischl, Charles Renard et al. (+25 more)
papers.summary: Contrails, or ice crystals formed by aircraft emissions, can still occur even when soot levels are low. This finding challenges current understanding of contrail formation and may impact climate modeling.

papers.title: Investigating the reproducibility of the social and behavioural sciences
papers.authors: Olivia Miske, Anna Lou Abatayo, Mason Daley, Mirka Dirzo, Nicholas Fox et al. (+123 more)
papers.summary: A study of over 600 papers in the social and behavioral sciences found that reproducibility has improved in recent years, particularly among journals that require data sharing.

papers.title: Stoichiometric FeTe is a superconductor
papers.authors: Zi-Jie Yan, Zihao Wang, Bing Xia, Stephen Paolini, Ying-Ting Chan et al. (+11 more)
papers.summary: Researchers discovered that stoichiometric FeTe is a superconductor, contradicting previous assumptions about its properties. This finding could lead to new materials with unique electrical properties.

papers.title: Investigating the replicability of the social and behavioural sciences
papers.authors: Andrew H. Tyner, Anna Lou Abatayo, Mason Daley, Samuel Field, Nicholas Fox et al. (+287 more)
papers.summary: A large-scale study found that about half of results from social and behavioral science journals can be replicated, highlighting the importance of rigorous testing and validation.

papers.title: Nanoscale transfer-printed full-colour ultrahigh-resolution quantum dot LEDs
papers.authors: Lihua Lin, Jie Wang, Hailong Hu, Haolin Luo, Yanbin Liu et al. (+8 more)
papers.summary: Researchers developed a method for manufacturing high-performance full-color ultrahigh-resolution quantum dot LEDs using a hard silicon template as a nanoimprinting stamp combined with inverted transfer printing.

papers.title: Angle evolution of the superconducting phase diagram in twisted bilayer WSe<sub>2</sub>
papers.authors: Yinjie Guo, John Cenker, Ammon Fischer, Daniel Muñoz-Segovia, Jordan Pack et al. (+11 more)
papers.summary: A study found that superconductivity in twisted bilayer WSe2 evolves smoothly with twist angle and emerges near Fermi surface reconstruction, linking previously distinct phase diagrams.

papers.title: Flexible ensheathment of axons enables myelination of complex CNS networks
papers.authors: Cody L. Call, Sarah A. Neely, Jason J. Early, Owen G. James, Lida Zoupi et al. (+6 more)
papers.summary: Researchers discovered that the rate of axon ensheathment varies within individual myelinating processes, resulting in chains of myelin sheaths connected by bridges.

papers.title: Evolution of pandemic cholera at its global source
papers.authors: Amber Barton, Mokibul Hassan Afrad, Alyce Taylor-Brown, Nisha Singh, Chetan Thakur et al. (+9 more)
papers.summary: A study found that the seventh cholera pandemic, driven by El Tor Vibrio cholerae, evolved separately in Bangladesh and India, with the Ganges Basin acting as the global source.

papers.title: Investigating the analytical robustness of the social and behavioural sciences
papers.authors: Balazs Aczel, Barnabas Szaszi, Harry T. Clelland, Marton Kovacs, Felix Holzmeister et al. (+489 more)
papers.summary: Researchers investigated the analytical robustness of social and behavioural science claims, finding that only 34% of reanalyses closely matched the original results.

papers.title: Dual-symmetry-guided assembly of complex lattices
papers.authors: Huang Fang, Xiaotian Li, Wensi Sun, Chengxin Wang, Nuo Chen et al. (+5 more)
papers.summary: A dual-symmetry-guided strategy assembles complex Archimedean lattices and quasicrystalline structures, providing a general route to complex-symmetry materials.

papers.title: A chelicera-bearing arthropod reveals the Cambrian origin of chelicerates
papers.authors: Rudy Lerosey-Aubril, Javier Ortega-Hernández
papers.summary: A new arthropod species, Megachelicerax cousteaui, is discovered with massive chelicerae and other unique features, revealing the Cambrian origin of chelicerates.

papers.title: An enteric neuron ionotropic receptor regulates salt stress resistance
papers.authors: Jihye Yeon, Jinmahn Kim, Koji Sato, Stephen Nurrish, Laurie Chen et al. (+8 more)
papers.summary: The I3 pharyngeal enteric neuron in Caenorhabditis elegans detects high-salt conditions and regulates genes related to salt stress resistance.

papers.title: Deconstruction of a spino-brain–spinal cord circuit that drives chronic pain
papers.authors: Qian Wang, Joo Han Lee, Gregory Nachtrab, Yuan Yuan, Lei Yuan et al. (+5 more)
papers.summary: A circuit between the spinal cord and brain regions drives chronic pain in mice, with a key role for spinal-cord-projecting neurons.

papers.title: General scales unlock AI evaluation with explanatory and predictive power
papers.authors: Lexin Zhou, Lorenzo Pacchiardi, Fernando Martínez-Plumed, Katherine M. Collins, Yael Moros-Daval et al. (+21 more)
papers.summary: A new methodology, General Scales, evaluates AI systems' capabilities and predicts their performance using rubrics capturing cognitive and intellectual demands.

papers.title: Hydroxy-induced cobalt oxides for syngas to light olefins
papers.authors: Yu Han, Jiafeng Yu, Jian Wei, Chuanyan Fang, Jianxiang Han et al. (+7 more)
papers.summary: A new catalyst made from cobalt oxides and hydroxy promoters improves the efficiency and sustainability of converting syngas to light olefins.

papers.title: Active dissociation of intracortical spiking and high gamma activity
papers.authors: Tianhao Lei, Michael R. Scheid, Robert D. Flint, Joshua I. Glaser, Marc W. Slutzky
papers.summary: A brain-machine interface study in monkeys reveals the biophysical underpinnings of cortical high gamma-band activity.

papers.title: Reproducibility and robustness of economics and political science research
papers.authors: Abel Brodeur, Derek Mikola, Nikolai Cook, Lenka Fiala, Thomas Brailey et al. (+342 more)
papers.summary: A study of economics and political science research finds high levels of robustness and reproducibility, with data and code-sharing requirements improving results.

papers.title: Catalyst diverts course of industrial process to make valuable hydrocarbons
papers.authors: Jingxiu Xie
papers.summary: A new catalyst with two cobalt components could lead to sustainable processes for manufacturing light olefins, key ingredients in plastics and detergents.

papers.title: Lift off! Artemis II mission sends humans to the Moon — opening a new era of exploration
papers.authors: Alexandra Witze
papers.summary: The Artemis II mission sends humans to the Moon, marking a new era of exploration and paving the way for future lunar missions.

papers.title: Super-potent opioids could be safer-than-expected alternatives to conventional painkillers
papers.authors: Victor Mathis, Emmanuel Darcq
papers.summary: Researchers found that modified nitazenes, which are 1,000 times stronger than morphine, may be safer alternatives to conventional painkillers. These super-potent opioids showed few adverse effects in rodents, renewing their potential for pain relief.

papers.title: Is social media addictive? Why a formal diagnosis is still out of reach
papers.authors: Dar Meshi
papers.summary: Due to limited methods and a lack of guidance on diagnostic criteria, scientists are reluctant to use the term 'addiction' when describing social media's impact.

papers.title: Struggling to focus on research when the world is ‘on fire’? Some ways to cope
papers.authors: Diana Kwon
papers.summary: Researchers are struggling with the emotional toll of keeping up with distressing news, which is affecting their mental health and scientific work.

papers.title: Hallucinated citations are polluting the scientific literature. What can be done?
papers.authors: Miryam Naddaf, Elizabeth Quill
papers.summary: An analysis suggests that tens of thousands of publications from 2025 may include invalid references generated by AI, polluting the scientific literature.

papers.title: Daily briefing: The countdown to NASA’s Artemis II Moon mission launch
papers.authors: Flora Graham
papers.summary: The upcoming NASA Artemis II Moon mission will conduct various scientific experiments, but some researchers are not concerned about its significance.

papers.title: Cutting aircraft soot emissions is not enough to curb contrail clouds
papers.authors: Unknown authors
papers.summary: Contrail clouds formed by jet engines contribute to global warming. However, in-flight measurements show that contrails are formed through different mechanisms depending on soot-emission levels and fuel composition.

papers.title: Half of social-science studies fail replication test in years-long project
papers.authors: Nicola Jones
papers.summary: A massive project to replicate social-science studies found that half of them failed the replication test. This reinforces concerns about science credibility but raises hope for solutions.

papers.title: Pincer movement: fossil pushes origins of chelicerate arthropods back to the Cambrian period
papers.authors: James C. Lamsdell
papers.summary: Fossil evidence reveals the evolution of chelicerate arthropods, which include spiders and scorpions. The findings push back the origins of this group to the Cambrian period.

papers.title: ‘Replication games’ test the robustness of social-science studies
papers.authors: Unknown authors
papers.summary: A large-scale assessment of social-science studies found that most were computationally reproducible, but about a quarter contained coding errors. The study highlights the importance of robustness checks.

papers.title: More self-reflection in research can lead to better science
papers.authors: Unknown authors
papers.summary: A package of papers highlights the value of self-reflection in research, particularly in the social and behavioural sciences. The goal is to promote reproducible, replicable, and robust findings.

papers.title: Why science has a credibility problem — and how to address it
papers.authors: Nicola Jones
papers.summary: A large-scale study examines the reproducibility and replicability of published results in social sciences, highlighting lessons learned from this meta-research project.

papers.title: Huge meta-research project puts claims in social-science papers to the test
papers.authors: Alfredo Sánchez-Tójar, Jelte M. Wicherts, Robb Willer
papers.summary: A historical review discusses the development and impact of synthetic fuels, specifically the Fischer–Tropsch synthesis, which has shaped history by providing a way to make liquid fuels from coal.

papers.title: 100 years of synthetic fuels
papers.authors: Ferdi Schüth
papers.summary: An author correction is made to a previous paper on ambient pressure superconductivity in thin film La3Ni2O7, correcting signatures of this phenomenon.

papers.title: Author Correction: Signatures of ambient pressure superconductivity in thin film La<sub>3</sub>Ni<sub>2</sub>O<sub>7</sub>
papers.authors: Eun Kyo Ko, Yijun Yu, Yidi Liu, Lopa Bhatt, Jiarui Li et al. (+9 more)
papers.summary: A study examines the structure of the mouse cytoplasmic lattice, providing new insights into cellular organization and function.

papers.title: Structure of the mouse cytoplasmic lattice
papers.authors: Pengliang Chi, Xiang Wang, Jialu Li, Jingrui Huang, Sicheng Ju et al. (+13 more)
papers.summary: A commentary discusses the credibility problem in science, highlighting the need for improved research practices and transparency.

papers.title: Author Correction: Phenome-wide analysis of copy number variants in 470,727 UK Biobank genomes
papers.authors: Xueqing Zoe Zou, Fengyuan Hu, Haiyi Lou, Oliver S. Burren, Xiaoyin Li et al. (+88 more)
papers.summary: This paper corrects an error in the original publication of a large-scale analysis of copy number variants in UK Biobank genomes.

papers.title: Schrödinger’s carbon’: The misclassification of carbon capture and storage as standard mitigation
papers.authors: Rosalie Arendt
papers.summary: The paper argues that carbon capture and storage is not a standard mitigation strategy for climate change, challenging current assumptions.

papers.title: Bunny or bug?
papers.authors: Unknown authors
papers.summary: This paper appears to be a lighthearted article about unusual animal behaviors, with no scientific content.

papers.title: A solar system is born
papers.authors: Unknown authors
papers.summary: Astronomers have discovered a second planet forming around a young star, providing new insights into planetary formation.

papers.title: A responsible authorship culture is needed — it is a collective responsibility
papers.authors: Véronique Kiermer, Kirsten Bibbins-Domingo, Magdalena Skipper
papers.summary: The authors argue that a responsible authorship culture is needed in scientific publishing, emphasizing collective responsibility.

papers.title: AI used in warfare needs a strong ethical framework
papers.authors: Yana Suchikova, Serhii Nazarovets
papers.summary: The use of AI in warfare requires a strong ethical framework to ensure its responsible development and deployment.

papers.title: The hidden costs of ‘helpful’ AI
papers.authors: Sylvie Delacroix
papers.summary: Even when AI tools aid decision-making, they can quietly de-skill whole professions by limiting how uncertainties and values are debated.

papers.title: Artemis II mission is about to fly humans to the Moon — here’s the science they’ll do
papers.authors: Alexandra Witze
papers.summary: The NASA Artemis II mission will take astronauts around the Moon for the first time in over 50 years, marking a significant step towards lunar exploration.

papers.title: Daily briefing: Are boys really in crisis? What the science says
papers.authors: Flora Graham
papers.summary: Concerns about boys being in crisis are widespread, but evidence suggests that these worries should be understood within the context of all young people.

papers.summary: A model-agnostic measure is proposed to quantify the reliability of language model-inferred protein, gene, and genome representations.

papers.title: An instrument named Cannoli

papers.summary: A lighthearted article shares the story of an instrument named Cannoli, highlighting scientists' affectionate naming conventions.

papers.title: Quantifying uncertainty in protein representations across models and tasks

papers.summary: A model-agnostic framework is proposed to measure uncertainty in protein embeddings and assess their biological relevance, improving model reliability.

papers.summary: A new method called ROCKET improves experimental structure determination by combining machine learning with X-ray crystallography and cryo-electron microscopy. This method uses a pretrained neural network to provide implicit structural knowledge, which is then integrated with experimental data.

papers.title: Two-dimensional materials for integrated sensing
papers.authors: Hangyu Xu, Zhihao Xu, Qinqi Ren, Yuan Meng, Sangmoon Han et al. (+4 more)
papers.summary: Two-dimensional materials have the potential to be used for in situ encoding and optoelectronic sensing in computing technologies. This review discusses the current state of integrated optoelectronic sensing based on 2D materials and future challenges.

papers.title: Bulk nano-heterointerface secures molecular contacts in perovskite solar cells
papers.authors: Yixin Luo, Jiahui Shen, Ke Zhao, Shenglong Chu, Yuan Tian et al. (+25 more)
papers.summary: A new design for perovskite solar cells improves their operational stability by strengthening the binding force between molecules and spherical silicon oxide nanoparticles. This is achieved through a bulk nano-heterointerface that increases the interfacial contact area.

papers.title: Hydrogels find their inner magnetism
papers.authors: Stephen J. Blundell
papers.summary: Hydrogels containing C=O groups and calcium cations show an unexpected paramagnetic effect, which may have biomedical applications. This finding could lead to new uses for hydrogels in medicine.

papers.title: Nanophotonic chip-space interfaces for multidimensional nonlinear optics
papers.authors: Dunzhao Wei, Bo Chen, Shuai Wan, Yixuan Wang, Jiantao Ma et al. (+13 more)
papers.summary: A new type of nanophotonic chip-space interface can generate on-demand nonlinear structured light, including tunable optical vortices and spatiotemporal vortex pulses. This is achieved through the use of thin-film lithium niobate microrings.

papers.title: Static magnetization switching in an artificial antiferromagnetic multilayer driven by a voltage-controlled magnetic anisotropy effect
papers.authors: Hiroyasu Nakayama, Takayuki Nozaki, Toshiki Yamaji, Tomohiro Nozaki, Hiroshi Imamura et al. (+1 more)
papers.summary: Researchers demonstrated static magnetization switching using voltage-controlled magnetic anisotropy without being sensitive to pulse widths. This method could improve data storage devices.

papers.title: Removing aluminium impurities in primary magnesium at an ultra-low cost
papers.authors: Rui Zheng, Bo Yang, Yue-Cun Wang, Bo-Yu Liu, Wei-Yi Yang et al. (+1 more)
papers.summary: A new method to remove aluminum impurities from magnesium at a low cost was discovered. This could enable large-scale production of high-purity magnesium.

papers.title: Option for transparent peer review
papers.authors: Unknown authors
papers.summary: Nature Materials now offers the option for transparent peer review, allowing authors to include reviewer comments and rebuttals with their publication.

papers.title: Tuning phonon transmission via single-atom substituents
papers.authors: Yuxuan Luan, Matthias Blaschke, Yuji Isshiki, Jian Guan, Fabian Pauly et al. (+2 more)
papers.summary: Researchers found that single-atom substituents can tune phonon transmission in molecular junctions, controlling thermal conductance by a factor of two.

papers.title: A toolkit for modeling cis-regulatory logic of enhancers at large scale
papers.authors: Unknown authors
papers.summary: A new software package, CREsted, was developed to model the regulatory logic of enhancers at a large scale. This could help decipher the genomic regulatory code.

papers.title: CREsted: modeling genomic and synthetic cell-type-specific enhancers across tissues and species
papers.authors: Niklas Kempynck, Seppe De Winter, Casper H. Blaauw, Vasileios Konstantakos, Eren Can Ekşi et al. (+10 more)
papers.summary: CREsted is a toolbox for analyzing, modeling, and designing cell-type-specific enhancers across various species. It's efficient and user-friendly.

papers.title: Synaptic connectomics by sequencing
papers.authors: Gilbert L. Henry, Anthony M. Zador
papers.summary: A new sequencing-based method maps synaptic connections by capturing paired neuronal barcodes from intact fragments, enabling comprehensive brain connectivity maps.

papers.title: Assessing uncertainty of sequence representations generated by protein language models
papers.authors: Unknown authors
papers.summary: A model-agnostic measure quantifies the reliability of language model-inferred protein, gene, and genome representations.

papers.title: An instrument named Cannoli
papers.authors: Vivien Marx
papers.summary: A lighthearted article shares the story of an instrument named Cannoli, highlighting scientists' affectionate nicknames for their equipment.

papers.title: Quantifying uncertainty in protein representations across models and tasks
papers.authors: R. Prabakaran, Yana Bromberg
papers.summary: A model-agnostic framework measures uncertainty in protein embeddings and assesses biological relevance to improve model reliability.

papers.title: AlphaFold as a prior: experimental structure determination conditioned on a pretrained neural network
papers.authors: Alisia Fadini, Minhuan Li, Airlie J. McCoy, Suresh Banjara, Hiroki Okumura et al. (+8 more)
papers.summary: A new method called ROCKET combines machine learning models with experimental data to improve the accuracy of protein structure determination. This approach uses implicit structural knowledge from a pre-trained neural network to enhance experimental results. The study shows that ROCKET can improve the quality of protein structures determined by X-ray crystallography and cryo-electron microscopy. This could have significant implications for understanding protein function and behavior.
